title: Discriminative vs. Generative Models
concept: Every model built so far — classifiers, detectors, segmenters — is discriminative: it looks at an existing input and predicts something about it. A generative model instead learns to produce brand-new examples that resemble the training data.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What do image classifiers, object detectors, and segmentation models all have in common in terms of their fundamental task?

options.text: They all analyze an existing image and output a prediction about it — a label, a box, or a mask
They all generate brand-new images from scratch that never existed before
They only work on video footage, never on still photos

exercises.explain: Exactly! Every computer vision model you've built so far takes something real as input and predicts something about it.

cards.title: Every model so far has played detective
cards.body: Show a classifier a photo, and it tells you: cat or dog. Show a detector a street scene, and it draws boxes around pedestrians and cars. Show a segmenter an MRI, and it outlines the tumor. Every single model in this course has been a detective, examining evidence that already exists. But what if a model could dream up evidence that never existed at all?

cards.title: Discriminative vs. generative
cards.body: A discriminative model draws boundaries between existing categories, answering "what is this?" — that's every classifier, detector, and segmenter you've built. A generative model instead learns the pattern of the training data well enough to produce a brand-new example, answering "can you create something new that looks like this?"

cards.title: A judge vs. a novelist
cards.body: A discriminative model is like a judge at a talent show: handed a contestant, it scores or sorts them into a category. A generative model is like a novelist: handed nothing but a blank page, it invents a brand-new character from imagination — informed by every story it has ever read, but copying none of them exactly.

cards.title: Same photos, two very different jobs
cards.body: Train a discriminative model on skin-lesion photos, and it outputs "benign" or "malignant" for a real photo. Train a generative model on the very same photos, and it can instead produce a brand-new, entirely synthetic lesion photo that no real patient ever had — one that never existed until the model created it.

cards.title: Generative isn't just "smarter" discriminative
cards.body: It's tempting to think a generative model is simply a fancier classifier. In reality they solve different problems entirely: a generative model might be terrible at labeling a photo, because its whole job was never to sort things — it was to make convincing new things.

cards.title: What each type answers
cards.body: Two very different jobs, side by side:

left.text:
Input: a real example
Output: a label, box, or mask
Answers "What is this?"
e.g. classifiers, detectors, segmenters

right.text:
Input: noise or nothing at all
Output: a brand-new example
Answers "Can you make one?"
e.g. new faces, new art, new data

exercises.prompt: Which question does a discriminative model answer?

options.text: What is this input? (a label, a box, or a category)
Can you create a brand-new example that looks like this?
What would this look like if it had never existed before?
How would you invent a new version of this from scratch?

exercises.explain: Correct! Discriminative models examine an existing input and predict something about it.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A generative model's main goal is to sort inputs into existing categories.
exercises.explain: False. Sorting into categories is what discriminative models do. Generative models create new examples instead.

exercises.sentence: A discriminative model predicts a label for existing data, while a generative model creates ___ examples.
exercises.explain: A generative model's output is a brand-new example, not a label attached to an existing one.

exercises.prompt: Which of these describes a generative model's task?

options.text: Producing a brand-new synthetic face photo that doesn't belong to any real person
Drawing bounding boxes around cars in a street photo

exercises.explain: Correct! Only the synthetic face is a genuinely new example — the others are predictions about existing photos.

exercises.prompt: A hospital runs two AI systems. System A looks at real MRI scans and labels each one "tumor" or "healthy." System B never looks at a real patient scan as input — instead, it produces brand-new, realistic-looking synthetic MRI images from scratch. Which system is generative, and why?

options.text: System B, because it creates new data resembling the training distribution rather than classifying existing input
System A, because labeling scans requires more intelligence than creating images
Both systems are generative, since both use neural networks
Neither system is generative, since both were trained on real patient data

exercises.explain: Exactly! System B is generative — it manufactures new examples instead of judging existing ones. Next: what kinds of things can generative models actually create?
